---
title: "LRPITT15b: Taught vs not-taught receptive vocabulary (generalisation contrast)"

date: last-modified
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss Z"

format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    number-sections: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-links:
      - repo
---

::: {.callout-note}
Drafted by a LLM-based AI tool (Codex/GPT-5).
:::

{{< include _partials/_header.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_setup.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_gate_badge.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_key_findings.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_reading_guide.qmd >}}

## Overview

LRPITT15b is the *receptive*-modality companion to LRPITT15: it tests whether the randomised intervention effect is larger for directly-taught than not-taught receptive vocabulary, one component of the broader generalisation claim. Two Block 1 receptive vocabulary outcomes are fitted together as factorised marginals over the randomised window (t1 $\rightarrow$ t2):

- `TR` — directly-taught target words (`b1retau`, $N = 24$);
- `UR` — not-taught comparison words (`b1rent`, $N = 12$).

The headline quantity is $\mathrm{AME}_{\mathrm{TR}} - \mathrm{AME}_{\mathrm{UR}}$ on the expected proportion-correct risk-difference scale. A positive value means the intervention increased expected proportion correct more for taught receptive words than for not-taught receptive words. It does **not** by itself show that generalisation was limited: that conclusion also requires the marginal UR effect to be small against a substantively defined negligible-effect threshold. The conditional logit-coefficient difference is secondary. The per-outcome models are LRPITT01 (TR) and LRPITT03 (UR).

## Structure

For child $i$ and outcome $k \in \{\mathrm{TR}, \mathrm{UR}\}$:

$$
\eta_{k,i} = \alpha_k + \tau_k G_i
           + \gamma^{\text{own}}_k\, \text{logit}_*(k_{\text{pre},i}, N_k)
           + \gamma^{A}_k\, A_{\text{std},i},
$$

with a per-outcome Beta-Binomial likelihood and dispersion $\kappa_k$. As in LRPITT15, the 2×2 LKJ residual-correlation block is **off**, so this is a product of outcome marginals rather than a dependence-aware joint posterior. This avoids the poor mixing seen previously but does not make the contrast uncertainty automatically conservative. The current contrast is therefore an exploratory factorised sensitivity result, not calibrated paired inference; confirmation requires a paired child-level randomisation-inference/permutation analysis, bootstrap, sandwich, or defensible dependence-model analysis.

::: {.callout-note title="Denominators and caveats"}

- Taught ($\mathrm{TR}$): $N = 24$ — documented (Burgoyne et al., 2012, Table 3).
- Not-taught ($\mathrm{UR}$): $N = 12$ — **observed maximum, not documented** and flagged unconfirmed; the probability-scale interpretation is conditional on that scoring maximum being correct.
- A positive taught-minus-not-taught difference establishes only that the taught effect is larger. Assess whether transfer is small from the marginal UR AME against a substantively defined negligible-effect threshold.
- $n \approx 54$; the causal claim is limited to the randomised window. The headline contrast compares expected proportions correct, not raw words.
- The factorised fit does not estimate within-child residual covariance. `use_residual_correlation=True` remains available as a model sensitivity but mixed poorly in the earlier fit; until a separate calibrated dependence analysis is completed, the report must not make a definitive paired generalisation claim.
- PSIS-LOO leaves out a child by summing that child's observed outcome-cell log likelihoods.

:::

![Model diagram](model_graph.png){#fig-model .lightbox fig-align="left"}

{{< include _partials/_results_joint.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_priors.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_prior_predictive.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_technical.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_footer.qmd >}}
